evals.name: order-service-domain-model-layers
evals.prompt: I'm building a new order management service. It needs to handle order placement, payment processing, inventory reservation, and send confirmation emails. Multiple teams will consume it via REST API and also via a message queue. We expect complex pricing rules that change quarterly. How should I structure the application?
evals.expected_output: Should recommend Domain Model + Service Layer, discuss Hexagonal Architecture as a possibility, recommend DTOs for the API boundary, and suggest Domain Events for the email/inventory side effects. Should reference the relevant decision points and explain the progression.
expectations: Recommends Domain Model for the business logic given complex pricing rules
Recommends Service Layer given multiple interfaces (REST + queue)
Discusses Domain Events for side effects (email, inventory)
Mentions DTOs or boundary objects for the REST API
Does NOT recommend Transaction Script for this level of complexity
References the YAGNI gate or progression path
Discusses data access strategy (Repository or similar given domain complexity)

evals.name: logic-in-flask-handlers-refactor
evals.prompt: I have a Flask app where all the business logic is in the route handlers. Validation, database queries, price calculations, email sending -- all in 200-line handler functions. There are about 15 endpoints and the logic is getting duplicated between the REST API and a CLI tool we added last month. How should I restructure this?
evals.expected_output: Should identify the smell (business logic in controllers), recommend extracting a Domain Model or at minimum a Service Layer to share logic between REST and CLI, and provide a progression path. Should NOT recommend jumping straight to Hexagonal Architecture or Event Sourcing.
expectations: Identifies the architectural smell (business logic in route handlers / controllers)
Recommends extracting business logic out of the handlers
Suggests Service Layer as a solution for the REST+CLI duplication problem
Discusses whether a Domain Model is warranted given the complexity
Does NOT recommend Event Sourcing or CQRS for this scenario
Provides incremental advice rather than a full rewrite
Addresses the data access concern (queries mixed into business logic)

evals.name: crud-tool-yagni-over-engineering
evals.prompt: I'm building an internal tool for my team of 3 people to track experiment results. It's basically CRUD -- create experiments, log results, view reports. Postgres database, maybe 5 tables. Should I implement a Repository pattern with Domain Events and CQRS to keep things clean from the start?
evals.expected_output: Should firmly push back via the YAGNI gates. For a 3-person CRUD tool with 5 tables, Transaction Script with direct ORM access is the right answer. Repository, Domain Events, and CQRS are all overkill. Should explain why without being dismissive.
expectations: Recommends against Repository pattern for this use case
Recommends against Domain Events for this use case
Recommends against CQRS for this use case
Suggests Transaction Script or direct ORM as the appropriate approach
Explains WHY these patterns are overkill (not just says they are)
References the YAGNI gate concept
Does not recommend adding patterns 'for future growth' or 'just in case'
